excerpt: Regarding the interpretation of the verse 'I know what you reveal and I know what you conceal,' Imam Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq narrates from his father, Maulānā Muḥammad al-Bāqir, that what the angel revealed was the outward declaration, and what they concealed was the hidden doubt in their hearts.

The alternative interpretation describes how when Allāh fashioned Ādam's form from clay, the angels marveled at his beauty, but Iblīs questioned his nature, finding him hollow within, yet harboring malice known only to the Originator.

When the angels confessed their error, Allāh revealed to them the secrets of their hearts. They repented and circumambulated the Throne seven times. Allāh commanded them to construct a sanctuary on earth where the wayward descendants of Ādam could gather and be forgiven.
source_location_hint: Narrative of angels' repentance and construction of the Kaʿba

excerpt: Iblīs was called one of the disbelievers because he knew well that Ādam surpassed him in capacity, as demonstrated to all the angels, yet deliberately veiled this truth from his consciousness, becoming a denier.
The garden (al-janna) in which Ādam and Ḥawwāʾ were placed signifies the station of prophethood connected to revelation, pure and subtle in essence. All stages of prayer are also called gardens.

Iblīs challenged Imam al-Hunayd, swearing that since he had fallen from his position because of Ādam's elevation, he would ensure none of Ādam's progeny attained his former station, declaring war against the descendants until the Qāʾim appears.

Maulānā ʿAlī teaches that Ādam was deceived because Iblīs swore a false oath. In the intellectual realm of Ādam and Ḥawwāʾ, falsehood had no place; they knew only truth. When Shaytān swore to them, they believed him, for false oath-taking was impossible for them.
source_author: al-Sharīf al-Rāḍī (transmitting from ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib)

excerpt: When Ādam and Ḥawwāʾ tasted the forbidden fruit, their nakedness was revealed. They tried to cover themselves with the leaves of Paradise, but only the fig tree took mercy upon them and offered its leaves.
source_work: Taʾwīl al-Daʾāʾim or Kitāb al-ʿAlim wa-l-Ghulām
source_author: Kirmānī or Jaʿfar ibn Manṣūr al-Yamān

excerpt: The Torah records that when Ādam and Ḥawwāʾ were naked and afraid in the garden, they heard Allāh's footsteps. Ādam blamed the woman, the woman blamed the serpent, and the serpent said Iblīs entered its belly. The Imām then explained that the prohibition was for their benefit, as they were not yet capable of comprehending those sciences.
source_location_hint: Narrative of Ādam's transgression and repentance

excerpt: The tree in the spiritual sense refers to knowledge of realities forbidden to the unworthy, particularly the sciences of the Imams that confer eternity and liberation; these cannot be acquired without obedience to and affiliation with the Imams, and are unveiled at the proper hour.
source_work: Inferred from Kirmani's al-Riyad and al-Aqwal al-Dhahabiyya on esoteric exegesis of Quranic narratives
source_location_hint: Doctrine of taʾwīl (esoteric interpretation) of scriptural stories as shadow-narratives of spiritual realities

excerpt: In another sense, the tree was Iblis himself; he was a great preacher and teacher of all angels, but refused to prostrate before Adam and was expelled. The prohibition against eating the fruit means Adam was forbidden to reveal to Iblis any secret of the daʿwa (the summons).
source_work: Kirmani's doctrine on the hierarchy of emanations and ranks (ḥudūd)
source_location_hint: Commentary on the meaning of obedience and the breaking of hierarchy in cosmic order

excerpt: Adam's children are among the rank of al-mustajībīn (the respondents), whose proper station is upon the earth, not in Paradise. Since respondents are not people of Paradise, the earth is their abode; therefore Adam's descendants were not created in Heaven.
source_work: Kirmani's exposition of the doctrine of ranks and hierarchical stations
source_location_hint: Theory of cosmic hierarchy: mubdiʿ (originator), munbaʿ (emanator), and graded limits (ḥudūd)

excerpt: A prophet cannot be a wrongdoer; Adam possessed a group of sinful souls upon whom retribution was executed. Adam's lower veil (animal soul) erred and descended to the islands; his higher veil (rational soul) remained among the noble. In every cycle (dawr) there appears an Adam who errs.
source_work: Kirmani's metaphysical exposition of the twofold nature of the soul and cyclic manifestation
source_location_hint: Doctrine of the apparent and hidden aspects of prophets and Imams across cycles

excerpt: The Second Emanation (al-munbaʿ al-thānī) received the seven words of the First Emanation through repentance and was restored to the spiritual ranks. Those who imitated him in the spiritual world and equated with him were likewise elevated; those who obeyed Iblis were cast into Hell.
source_work: Kirmani's exposition of the Second Emanation and its fall and restoration
source_location_hint: Cosmological narrative of the realm of intellects (ʿālam al-ʿuqūl)

excerpt: Adam and Hawwa received the blessed names—the attributes of the Five Pure Ones (Muhammad, Ali, Fatima, al-Hasan, al-Husayn)—through which they repented and sought the Imam's forgiveness. The Imam accepted their repentance and restored them to the station of prophethood.
source_work: Doctrinal synthesis attributed to Hazrat Zia Sahib and transmitted through Kirmani
source_location_hint: The Five Sublime Names as vehicles of divine mercy and restoration

excerpt: Revelation descends to the Natiq (the Speaker) through five intermediaries: the Pen, the Tablet, Israfil, Michael, Gabriel; from the Natiq those sciences are transmitted to the five spiritual limits beneath him, and from them to the believers. The sixth is he who receives—the believer.
source_work: Kirmani's doctrine of the celestial mediation and chain of transmission (silsila)
source_location_hint: Hierarchical ordering of emanative and receptive limits in the cosmos

excerpt: Adam appointed Seth (Shith) as his successor, naming him Hiba Allah (the gift of God), and entrusted him in secret with all the sciences of esoteric exegesis and the realities of divine truth and the affairs of the daʿwa. From Seth onward his descendants preserved the chain of Imamate and Prophethood until the age of Noah.
source_work: Kirmani's account of the continuation of Prophethood and Imamate through the line of Seth
source_location_hint: Doctrine of the protected line (sitr) of prophets and Imams

excerpt: Adam is the symbolic embodiment of the opening verse of Surat al-Hamd, the founder of the divine cycle. He is the first being whose wilāya God made obligatory and the source of the binding obligation of guardianship. The Sharīʿa of Muhammad, destined to emerge after six thousand years, was founded and established by him.
source_work: Kirmani's doctrine of the foundational role of Adam in inaugurating the cycles (adwār) of prophethood
source_location_hint: Symbolism of Adam as the prototype of prophethood and wilāya across ages
